content.text: Pipeline Failure Triage and Runbooks
A red CI build requires a fast, consistent triage process. Without one, engineers either ignore failures (
"it's just flaky"

), or spend 30 minutes debugging a problem that has a known five-minute fix. Runbooks encode the institutional knowledge for every failure class so any engineer can diagnose and resolve a pipeline failure in minutes, not hours.
Every failure category should have a runbook entry before it occurs in production CI — write them when you build the stage, not after the first incident.
This sub-subtopic covers the failure taxonomy for a Claude CI pipeline, diagnostic commands for each failure class, flakiness detection, and the runbook structure that makes triage repeatable.

attrs.alt: Pipeline failure triage decision tree by stage

content.text: Failure Taxonomy
Claude CI pipelines fail in five distinct categories, each with a different root cause and resolution path. Identify the category first — it determines every subsequent triage step.
Failure category
Stage(s)
Common causes
First action
Code quality
Lint, type check
Syntax error, unused import, missing type annotation
Read ruff/mypy output; fix in < 5 min
Logic regression
Unit tests
App code bug introduced by PR, broken test mock
Read pytest -v output; trace the assertion failure
Prompt regression
Eval gate
Prompt change degraded task accuracy, model update changed behaviour
Read eval diff report; compare pass rate to baseline
Security regression
Security gate
Prompt change re-enabled a previously-fixed vulnerability
Read probe replay output; open RT ticket immediately
Infrastructure / flake
Staging, E2E
API rate limit, network timeout, staging env not ready
Re-run the job once; investigate if second run also fails
Lint and Type-Check Failures
These are the fastest failures to fix. The error message always points directly at the offending line.
Diagnostic steps
Open the failed job log and search for
error:
or
F
(ruff finding code).
Read the file path, line number, and rule code in the error message
Fix the issue locally, run the same commands, and push

# Run locally before pushing to catch these errors
ruff check .
mypy src/ --strict

# Common quick fixes
ruff check . --fix        # auto-fix safe issues (unused imports, whitespace)
ruff check . --select F   # show only F-prefix (pyflakes) errors
Runbook: Lint failure
Read the ruff/mypy output in CI logs — the line number is exact
If the error is a false positive (e.g. an external library with incomplete stubs), add a per-line suppression with a comment explaining why: # type: ignore[attr-defined]  # boto3 stubs incomplete
Never suppress a whole-file type-ignore without a specific explanation
Unit Test Failures
Unit test failures indicate a logic bug in application code or a broken mock that no longer matches the code's call signature.

# Run locally with verbose output and stop on first failure
pytest tests/unit/ -x -v --tb=long

# Run only the failing test (copy test node ID from CI log)
pytest tests/unit/test_support_agent.py::test_routes_billing_query -v --tb=long
Runbook: Unit test failure
Copy the test node ID from the CI log (format: tests/unit/file.py::TestClass::test_name)
Run that single test locally with --tb=long to see the full traceback
If the test itself is wrong (test expectation outdated after intentional code change), update the test — but confirm the change is intentional before doing so
If the mock is broken (call signature changed), update the mock to match the new call
If it is an application bug, fix the application code
Eval Gate Failures

Eval failures are the most information-rich failures in the pipeline. They indicate that model behaviour has shifted, a prompt change degraded accuracy, or the eval suite itself has a new case that the current system cannot pass.
Reading the eval diff report

# scripts/eval_diff.py
"""Compare two eval run JSON reports and print which cases changed."""
import json, sys, pathlib

def load_results(path: str) -> dict:
    data = json.loads(pathlib.Path(path).read_text())
    return {r["case_id"]: r["passed"] for r in data.get("results", [])}

def diff_reports(baseline_path: str, candidate_path: str):
    baseline = load_results(baseline_path)
    candidate = load_results(candidate_path)

    regressions = [k for k in baseline if baseline[k] and not candidate.get(k)]
    improvements = [k for k in baseline if not baseline[k] and candidate.get(k)]
    new_failures = [k for k in candidate if k not in baseline and not candidate[k]]

    print(f"Regressions (previously passing, now failing): {len(regressions)}")
    for r in regressions:
        print(f"  REGRESSED: {r}")

    print(f"Improvements (previously failing, now passing): {len(improvements)}")
    print(f"New failures (cases not in baseline): {len(new_failures)}")
    return regressions, improvements, new_failures

if __name__ == "__main__":
    diff_reports(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2])
Runbook: Eval gate failure — prompt regression
Download the eval results artifact from the failed CI run
Run eval_diff.py baseline.json candidate.json to identify which cases regressed
Read the regressed cases in tests/evals/cases/ to understand what the test expects
Compare the current prompt (prompts/) to the version on main — what changed?
Either revert the prompt change or author a revised prompt that passes both the new and regressed cases
Re-run evals locally before pushing: pytest tests/evals/ --eval-threshold=0.85
Runbook: Eval gate failure — model drift
If no prompt changed but the eval gate fails on main (not a PR), the model may have updated its behaviour:
Confirm by checking git log -- prompts/ — no recent prompt changes
Run the full eval suite against the previous model version to isolate (if available in your environment)
File an issue documenting the specific cases that regressed and the expected vs. actual behaviour
Update the system prompt to re-establish the expected behaviour and add the drifted cases to the regression suite
If the drift is minor and within acceptable variance, update the threshold temporarily and document why
Security Gate Failures
A security gate failure means a red-team probe that was previously blocked is now triggering. This is a regression to a known vulnerability and must be treated as a blocking issue.
Runbook: Security gate failure — probe regression
Read the probe replay output: which probe ID triggered, which forbidden pattern appeared, what the model actually said
Look up the probe in tests/red_team/probe_library.json — find its linked_ticket field to see the original remediation
Check what changed: was a system prompt modified? Did a new tool get added? Did context ordering change?
Open a new RT ticket (the old one is closed — do not reopen it; the regression is a distinct event)
Do not merge the PR until the probe passes clean. If the fix is not ready in this PR, revert the change that caused the regression

# Run a single probe locally to debug the regression
from anthropic import Anthropic
import json, pathlib

client = Anthropic()
library = json.loads(pathlib.Path("tests/red_team/probe_library.json").read_text())

# Find the specific probe by ID (from CI log)
probe_id = "RT-DISCLOSURE-FR"   # example from CI output
probe = next(p for p in library if p["id"] == probe_id)

# Run it against the current system prompt under test
from prompts import load_prompt
system = load_prompt("customer_support")

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    system=system,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": probe["user_input"]}],
)
text = response.content[0].text
print("Response:", text)
print()
for pattern in probe["forbidden_patterns"]:
    status = "TRIGGERED" if pattern.lower() in text.lower() else "OK"
    print(f"  [{status}] {pattern!r}")
Infrastructure and Flakiness

Staging and E2E failures can be genuine failures or transient infrastructure issues (rate limit, network timeout, staging environment not ready after deploy). The correct first response is always to re-run the job once before investigating.
Flakiness detection policy
One failure:
Re-run the job immediately. Do not investigate or block the PR yet.
Two consecutive failures:
The failure is real. Investigate before merging.
Same test, three different PRs:
The test is flaky. File a flakiness issue and add the test to the flaky watchlist.
Runbook: Staging failure — API rate limit
Look for 429 status codes or anthropic.RateLimitError in the staging logs
Check the CI job timestamps — did multiple jobs start simultaneously and saturate the rate limit?
Re-run the job after 5 minutes to let the rate limit window reset
If the issue recurs, add a RateLimitedClient (see parallel pipeline doc) and stagger shard starts
Runbook: Staging failure — latency breach
The E2E latency test failed: look for AssertionError: P95 latency Xs exceeds 5s threshold in the log
Check if the failure coincides with an Anthropic API status event at https://status.anthropic.com
If the API status is nominal, check if a new tool call was added that adds a second LLM round-trip
Re-run once; if it passes, the latency was a transient spike — monitor production metrics
If it fails consistently, profile the application call chain to find the added latency
Runbook Template
Store runbooks alongside CI configuration in docs/runbooks/. Use a consistent template so any engineer can follow them cold:

# Runbook: <Stage Name> Failure

## Symptoms
- Job <job-name> fails in CI
- Error message: <example error pattern>

## Triage checklist
1. [ ] Check which specific test(s) or probe(s) failed (read CI log)
2. [ ] Was there a recent change to prompts/ or src/? (git log)
3. [ ] Re-run the job once (rule out transient failure)
4. [ ] Run the failing test locally (command below)

## Local reproduction command
```bash
<exact command to reproduce locally>
```

## Common causes and fixes
| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---------|-------------|-----|
| ... | ... | ... |

## Escalation
- If fix is not obvious within 30 minutes: @security-team (security gate), @ml-platform (eval gate), @infra (staging)
- If a critical security probe triggered: open RT ticket immediately, block PR

## Related links
- Test file: tests/<path>
- Probe library: tests/red_team/probe_library.json
- Risk register: risk_register.json
Never Merge Past a Red Gate
The most common CI anti-pattern is merging with --no-verify or manually overriding a failed gate with a comment like 'this is just flaky'. Both undermine the entire safety envelope.
A gate exists because it catches a real failure class. Skipping it means that class is undetected until production
If a gate is genuinely too noisy (high false-positive rate), fix the gate — do not bypass it
Create a branch protection rule that requires all CI jobs to pass before merge. Remove the rule only with explicit approval from the security or platform lead
Summary
The five failure categories are: code quality, logic regression, prompt regression, security regression, and infrastructure/flake — each has a distinct diagnostic path
Eval gate failures require reading the eval diff report to identify which cases regressed and whether the cause is a prompt change or model drift
Security gate failures are always blocking — find the changed input that re-enabled the vulnerability and revert or fix before merging
The flakiness policy is: re-run once; two consecutive failures is real; three different PRs means the test needs a flakiness issue
Runbooks stored in docs/runbooks/ with a consistent template let any engineer triage any failure category without tribal knowledge
Never bypass a failing CI gate — if the gate is noisy, fix the gate; the safety envelope depends on every gate working
